quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for density?

options.text: Density = Volume ÷ Mass
Density = Mass ÷ Volume
Density = Mass × Volume
Density = Mass + Volume

quiz_questions.explanation: Density is calculated by dividing mass by volume: D = M ÷ V. This tells you how much matter is packed into a given amount of space.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the standard unit for density in the metric system?

options.text: grams per liter (g/L)
grams per cubic centimeter (g/cm³)
kilograms per meter (kg/m)
milliliters per gram (mL/g)

quiz_questions.explanation: The standard unit for density is grams per cubic centimeter (g/cm³) for solids and grams per milliliter (g/mL) for liquids. Since 1 cm³ = 1 mL, these units are equivalent.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the density of pure water at room temperature?

options.text: 10 g/cm³
0.1 g/cm³
1.0 g/cm³
100 g/cm³

quiz_questions.explanation: Pure water has a density of approximately 1.0 g/cm³ (or 1.0 g/mL) at room temperature. This value is useful as a reference — objects denser than 1.0 g/cm³ sink in water, and less dense objects float.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object has a mass of 30 g and a volume of 10 cm³. What is its density?

options.text: 300 g/cm³
3 g/cm³
0.33 g/cm³
20 g/cm³

quiz_questions.explanation: Density = Mass ÷ Volume = 30 g ÷ 10 cm³ = 3 g/cm³. This object is denser than water (1.0 g/cm³), so it would sink.

quiz_questions.question_text: If an object is less dense than water, what will it do when placed in water?

options.text: Sink to the bottom
Float on the surface
Dissolve completely
Stay in the middle

quiz_questions.explanation: Objects less dense than water (density < 1.0 g/cm³) float. Objects more dense than water (density > 1.0 g/cm³) sink. This is why wood floats and metal sinks.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you find the volume of a rectangular solid (box)?

options.text: Length + Width + Height
Length × Width × Height
Length × Width only
2 × (Length + Width)

quiz_questions.explanation: The volume of a rectangular solid is calculated by multiplying Length × Width × Height. For example, a box that is 5 cm × 3 cm × 2 cm has a volume of 30 cm³.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you measure the volume of an irregularly shaped object like a rock?

options.text: Measure its length, width, and height with a ruler and multiply
Use the water displacement method in a graduated cylinder
Weigh it on a balance and convert the mass to volume directly
Estimate its volume by comparing it to a known regular shape

quiz_questions.explanation: Water displacement works by submerging the object in water and measuring the volume of water it pushes aside. The difference between the water level before and after equals the object's volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: A graduated cylinder contains 50 mL of water. After a rock is added, the water level rises to 65 mL. What is the volume of the rock?

options.text: 65 mL
50 mL
15 mL
115 mL

quiz_questions.explanation: The volume of the rock equals the water displaced: 65 mL − 50 mL = 15 mL (or 15 cm³, since 1 mL = 1 cm³). The rock pushed the water up by 15 mL.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which object is the most dense?

options.text: A wooden block: 10 g, 20 cm³
A metal cube: 40 g, 5 cm³
A plastic ball: 15 g, 25 cm³
A foam block: 5 g, 50 cm³

quiz_questions.explanation: Calculate each density: Wood = 10/20 = 0.5 g/cm³, Metal = 40/5 = 8.0 g/cm³, Plastic = 15/25 = 0.6 g/cm³, Foam = 5/50 = 0.1 g/cm³. The metal cube at 8.0 g/cm³ is the most dense.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does changing the size of an object change its density?

options.text: Yes, bigger objects always have higher density than smaller objects
No, density is a property of the material, not the size of the object
Yes, smaller objects always have higher density than larger objects
It depends on whether the object is a solid, liquid, or a gas form

quiz_questions.explanation: Density is an intensive property — it does not change with size. A small piece of aluminum has the same density (2.7 g/cm³) as a large piece. Both mass and volume change proportionally.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is density?

options.text: A measure of how much mass is packed into a given volume of a substance
A measure of how large an object is compared to other similar objects
A measure of the total weight of an object using a spring scale device
A measure of how fast an object moves through space over a period of time

quiz_questions.explanation: Density measures how tightly matter is packed together — the amount of mass per unit of volume. A dense material (like lead) has a lot of mass in a small space, while a less dense material (like foam) has little mass in the same space.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is volume?

options.text: The amount of space that an object or substance takes up
The amount of matter contained within an object or substance
The force of gravity acting on an object near the surface
The distance from one side of an object to the other side

quiz_questions.explanation: Volume is the amount of three-dimensional space an object occupies. It is measured in cubic centimeters (cm³) for solids and milliliters (mL) or liters (L) for liquids.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'water displacement' mean?

options.text: A method of measuring volume by seeing how much water an object pushes aside
The process of removing water from a container by pouring it out through a spout
A technique for measuring the temperature of water using a digital thermometer
The method of filtering impurities from water before using it in experiments

quiz_questions.explanation: Water displacement measures volume by submerging an object in water and measuring how much the water level rises. The volume of water displaced equals the volume of the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'mass' mean?

options.text: The amount of matter contained in an object, measured in grams or kilograms
The amount of space an object takes up, measured in cubic centimeters
The force of gravity pulling on an object, measured in units of newtons
The speed at which an object moves, measured in meters per second

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg). Unlike weight, mass does not change with location — an object has the same mass on Earth as on the Moon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'buoyancy' mean?

options.text: The upward force exerted by a fluid on an object placed in it
The downward pull of gravity on every object near Earth's surface
The speed at which an object sinks to the bottom of a liquid
The total volume of water that a container is able to hold

quiz_questions.explanation: Buoyancy is the upward force that a fluid (liquid or gas) exerts on an object placed in it. If the buoyant force equals the object's weight, the object floats. If weight is greater, the object sinks.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sink' mean in terms of density?

options.text: An object sinks when its density is greater than the density of the surrounding fluid
An object sinks when its density is less than the density of the surrounding fluid
An object sinks when it has a very large volume regardless of its mass value
An object sinks only when it is made of metal and placed in a tank of water

quiz_questions.explanation: An object sinks when it is denser than the fluid it is placed in. For example, a steel ball (density ~7.8 g/cm³) sinks in water (density 1.0 g/cm³) because steel is denser than water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'float' mean in terms of density?

options.text: An object floats when its density is less than the density of the surrounding fluid
An object floats when its density is greater than the density of the surrounding fluid
An object floats only when it is hollow inside and filled with nothing but air
An object floats when it has a very small volume regardless of its mass value

quiz_questions.explanation: An object floats when it is less dense than the fluid. Wood (density ~0.5 g/cm³) floats on water (1.0 g/cm³) because wood is less dense. Oil floats on water for the same reason.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'cubic centimeter' (cm³)?

options.text: A unit of volume equal to the space inside a cube measuring 1 cm on each side
A unit of mass equal to one hundredth of a gram used for small objects
A unit of length used to measure very short distances between two points
A unit of temperature equal to one degree on the Celsius temperature scale

quiz_questions.explanation: A cubic centimeter (cm³) is the volume of a cube that measures 1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm. It is equivalent to 1 milliliter (mL). Both units are commonly used in science for measuring small volumes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'intensive property' mean?

options.text: A property that does not change based on the amount of substance present
A property that increases as the amount of substance increases over time
A property that requires intense heat to measure in a laboratory setting
A property that can only be observed using a very powerful microscope lens

quiz_questions.explanation: An intensive property does not depend on how much material you have. Density is an intensive property — a drop of water and a lake of water both have the same density (1.0 g/cm³). Temperature and color are also intensive properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Archimedes' principle' mean?

options.text: An object in a fluid is pushed up by a force equal to the weight of fluid it displaces
An object in a fluid always sinks to the bottom regardless of its density value
The mass of an object increases when it is submerged in a fluid like water
The volume of an object decreases when it is placed under water pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant (upward) force on a submerged object equals the weight of the fluid displaced. This principle explains why some objects float and others sink.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has a metal cube that is 2 cm × 2 cm × 2 cm and has a mass of 64 g. What is the density of the metal?

options.text: 32 g/cm³
8 g/cm³
16 g/cm³
4 g/cm³

quiz_questions.explanation: First find volume: 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 cm³. Then calculate density: 64 g ÷ 8 cm³ = 8 g/cm³. This density is close to iron/steel (7.87 g/cm³), so the metal could be steel.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a wooden block in water. It floats with about half of it below the surface. What can you conclude about its density?

options.text: The wood's density is about 2.0 g/cm³, twice the density of water
The wood's density is about 0.5 g/cm³, about half the density of water
The wood's density is exactly 1.0 g/cm³, equal to the density of water
You cannot determine anything about density from how an object floats

quiz_questions.explanation: If half the block is submerged, the wood's density is about half that of water, or approximately 0.5 g/cm³. The fraction of an object submerged equals the ratio of its density to the fluid's density.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has two cubes of the same size. Cube A has a mass of 20 g and Cube B has a mass of 60 g. Which cube is denser, and what does this tell you?

options.text: Cube A is denser because it is lighter and takes up less volume overall
Both are equally dense because they have the exact same volume and size
Cube B is denser because it has more mass in the same volume of space
Neither is denser because density depends only on shape, not on mass

quiz_questions.explanation: Since both cubes have the same volume, the one with more mass (Cube B, 60 g) is denser. This means Cube B's material has more matter packed into the same space, so it is likely a different and heavier material.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pours honey, water, and vegetable oil into a glass. The honey sinks to the bottom, water stays in the middle, and oil floats on top. What does this tell you about their densities?

options.text: Honey is the least dense, water is in the middle, and oil is the most dense
All three liquids have exactly the same density but different colors
Honey is the most dense, water is in the middle, and oil is the least dense
The order is random and would change if you poured them in a different order

quiz_questions.explanation: Liquids layer by density: the densest liquid (honey, ~1.4 g/cm³) sinks to the bottom, water (1.0 g/cm³) stays in the middle, and the least dense (oil, ~0.9 g/cm³) floats on top.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures a rock's mass as 45 g. She places it in a graduated cylinder with 30 mL of water, and the water rises to 45 mL. What is the rock's density?

options.text: 1.0 g/cm³
1.5 g/cm³
3.0 g/cm³
45 g/cm³

quiz_questions.explanation: Volume of rock = 45 mL − 30 mL = 15 mL = 15 cm³. Density = Mass ÷ Volume = 45 g ÷ 15 cm³ = 3.0 g/cm³. The rock is three times denser than water.

quiz_questions.question_text: A large steel ship floats on water even though steel is denser than water (7.8 g/cm³ vs. 1.0 g/cm³). How is this possible?

options.text: The ship's overall density including the air inside is less than water's density
Steel becomes less dense when it is shaped into the form of a large ship
Water becomes denser when a large object is placed on its surface in the ocean
Ships are not actually touching the water; they hover slightly above the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The ship's hollow shape encloses a large volume of air, making the overall density (steel + air) less than water. The ship displaces enough water for the buoyant force to support its weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has two objects with the same mass of 100 g. Object A has a volume of 50 cm³ and Object B has a volume of 25 cm³. Which object would sink faster if dropped in water?

options.text: Object A, because it has a larger volume and displaces more water overall
Object B, because it is denser with 4 g/cm³ compared to Object A's 2 g/cm³
Both would sink at the same rate since they have the same mass of 100 grams
Neither would sink because 100 g objects always float on the surface of water

quiz_questions.explanation: Object A: 100/50 = 2 g/cm³. Object B: 100/25 = 4 g/cm³. Both sink (both > 1 g/cm³), but Object B is denser and experiences less buoyant force relative to its weight, so it would tend to sink faster.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ice cube floats in water with about 10% of its volume above the surface. What does this tell you about the density of ice compared to liquid water?

options.text: Ice is slightly less dense than water, at about 0.9 g/cm³ compared to 1.0 g/cm³
Ice is much denser than water, which is why only a small fraction sticks out above
Ice and water have exactly the same density, which is always 1.0 g/cm³ precisely
Ice is much less dense than water at only about 0.1 g/cm³ compared to 1.0 g/cm³

quiz_questions.explanation: Since 90% is submerged and 10% is above, ice is about 90% as dense as water: approximately 0.9 g/cm³. This is why ice floats — a unique property of water that is essential for aquatic life in winter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student calculates the density of an unknown metal as 2.7 g/cm³. Her teacher says this matches aluminum. If the student had a larger piece of the same metal, what would its density be?

options.text: Higher than 2.7 g/cm³ because the piece is larger and has more mass
Still 2.7 g/cm³ because density does not change with the size of the sample
Lower than 2.7 g/cm³ because larger objects are always less dense overall
It cannot be determined without measuring the larger piece first in the lab

quiz_questions.explanation: Density is an intensive property — it stays the same regardless of sample size. A small piece and a large piece of aluminum both have a density of 2.7 g/cm³. This makes density useful for identifying materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has a mystery liquid. She measures 50 mL of it and finds it has a mass of 40 g. Will this liquid float on water, sink in water, or mix evenly?

options.text: It will sink because its density of 0.8 g/mL is less than water's density
It will float on water because its density of 0.8 g/mL is less than 1.0 g/mL
It will mix evenly because all liquids have the same density of 1.0 g/mL
It will sink because any liquid with a mass less than its volume will always sink

quiz_questions.explanation: Density = 40 g ÷ 50 mL = 0.8 g/mL. Since 0.8 g/mL < 1.0 g/mL (water), the mystery liquid is less dense than water and will float on top (assuming the liquids don't mix, like oil on water).
